sections.Theorem: This article is complete as far as it goes, but it could do with expansion. In particular: Make the interval general: [α..α+2π][ α . . α + 2 π ] for arbitrary αα , in order for consistency with the work on Fourier analysis as it is currently in progress. You can help Pr∞fWikiP r ∞ f W i k i by adding this information. To discuss this page in more detail, feel free to use the talk page. When this work has been completed, you may remove this instance of {{Expand}} from the code. If you would welcome a second opinion as to whether your work is correct, add a call to {{Proofread}} the page. Formulation 1 Let ff be a real function which is square-integrable over the interval(−π..π)( − π . . π ) . Let ff be expressed by the Fourier series: f(x)∼a02+∑n=1∞(ancosnx+bnsinnx)f ( x ) ∼ a 0 2 + ∑ n = ⁡ 1 ∞ ( a n cos ⁡ n x + b n sin ⁡ n x ) This article, or a section of it, needs explaining. In particular: What does ∼∼ mean? You can help Pr∞fWikiP r ∞ f W i k i by explaining it. To discuss this page in more detail, feel free to use the talk page. When this work has been completed, you may remove this instance of {{Explain}} from the code. Then: 1π∫π−π∣∣f2(x)∣∣dx=a022+∑n=1∞(an2+bn2)1 π ∫ − π π | f 2 ( x ) | d x = a 0 2 2 + ∑ n = ⁡ 1 ∞ ( a n 2 + b n 2 ) This article, or a section of it, needs explaining. In particular: Is this not |f(x)|2| f ( x ) | 2 ? I know these are the same but the latter is more common. You can help Pr∞fWikiP r ∞ f W i k i by explaining it. To discuss this page in more detail, feel free to use the talk page. When this work has been completed, you may remove this instance of {{Explain}} from the code. Formulation 2 Let ff be a real function which is square-integrable over the interval(−π..π)( − π . . π ) . Let ff be expressed by the Fourier series: f(x)=∑n=−∞∞cneinxf ( x ) = ∑ n = − ∞ ∞ c n e i n x where: cn=12π∫π−πf(t)e−intdtc n = 1 2 π ∫ − π π f ( t ) e − i n t d t Then: 12π∫π−π|f(x)|2dx=∑n=−∞∞|cn|21 2 π ∫ − π π | f ( x ) | 2 d x = ∑ n = − ∞ ∞ | c n | 2 Also known as Parseval's Theorem is also known as Parseval's Identity. Source of Name This entry was named for Marc-Antoine Parseval. Theorem


This article is complete as far as it goes, but it could do with expansion.
In particular: Make the interval general: [α..α+2π][α..α+2π] for arbitrary αα, in order for consistency with the work on Fourier analysis as it is currently in progress.
